title: The London Beer Flood
blurb: London, 1814: a brewery vat fails, other vats join the bit, and a neighborhood gets hit by a lethal wave of porter.
signatureLine: The domino effect, but make it carbonated.

cards.text: Horse Shoe Brewery stores porter in enormous wooden vats held together by heavy iron hoops.
cards.why: The huge containers concentrated a massive amount of liquid energy in one place.

cards.text: On October 17, 1814, one of the iron hoops on a giant vat breaks.
cards.why: A failed hoop weakened the structure that kept the pressurized beer contained.

cards.text: The damaged vat bursts and releases a huge volume of porter at once.
cards.why: Once the container failed, the beer escaped with enough force to become destructive.

cards.text: The blast knocks open other vats, multiplying the flood.
cards.why: One failure caused nearby storage failures, turning a spill into a chain reaction.

cards.text: A wave of beer pours through the brewery wall into the crowded St Giles neighborhood.
cards.why: The escaping liquid found the path of least resistance straight into nearby streets and homes.

cards.text: Basements and rooms flood, walls collapse, and eight people die.
cards.why: The flood was dangerous because it hit cramped housing and below-street spaces.

cards.text: A jury calls the disaster an accident, and the brewery receives a tax refund for the lost beer.
cards.why: The official aftermath shows how differently industrial responsibility was treated at the time.

trap.text: A crowded neighborhood wake was happening nearby that afternoon.
trap.whyNotCause: It may explain why people were gathered in one place, but it did not cause the vat, hoop, wall, or flood to fail.
